agents.help:
Agents are the autonomous units this LibreFang instance runs. Each agent has its own identity, prompt, tool set, memory, and budget — and lives as a row on this page until you delete it. From here you create new agents, edit existing ones, suspend / resume them, and clone working setups.

How to use this page:

1. Create. Click "Create agent" and pick a creation mode: form, template, or raw TOML. The form is the easiest way in; templates are pre-built archetypes (researcher, coder, …); TOML is the raw manifest if you already know what you want.

2. Inspect & edit. Click an agent row to open its detail panel — see its capabilities, hands, model, recent activity. Use "Edit" to change name, prompt, model, or tool policy. Use "Edit template TOML" to hand-edit the full manifest.

3. Lifecycle. Suspend pauses an agent (stops triggers, keeps state). Resume brings it back. Clone copies the manifest to a new agent so you can fork without breaking the original. Delete is permanent — sessions and memory go with it.

Typical scenarios:

  · Spinning up a per-domain agent (research / code / ops) and giving each its own channel and budget.
  · Forking an agent that works well, tweaking the prompt, A/B-testing the variants in parallel.
  · Suspending agents during quota crunches without losing their config or memory.

Things to know:

  · The agent.toml manifest controls runtime behaviour — session_mode, max_concurrent_invocations, max_history_messages, named workspaces. The form covers the common cases; the TOML editor is the escape hatch.
  · Identity files (SOUL.md, IDENTITY.md, …) live in {workspace}/.identity/. They migrate automatically on first spawn after upgrade.
  · Deleting an agent is irreversible. Suspend first if you might want it back.

providers.title: Providers
providers.subtitle: Manage LLM API connections and health.
providers.name: Name
providers.base_url_hint: Bare host:port URLs (e.g. http://192.168.1.10:11434) will get /v1 appended automatically for OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
providers.delete_confirm_message: Are you sure you want to remove the API key for this provider? The provider will become unconfigured.
providers.help:
Providers are the LLM vendor connections (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, …) — credentials, base URL, and health state. The model catalogue derives availability from this page, and every LLM call routes through a configured provider.

How to use this page:

1. Add or edit. "Add" creates a new provider connection — pick the kind, paste the API key, set base URL if you're hitting a self-hosted / proxy endpoint. Edit updates an existing one.

2. Test connectivity. "Batch test" pings every configured provider; per-row test does one. Successful test means the credentials authenticate and the endpoint is reachable.

3. Auto-select model. After test, "Auto-select model" picks a sane default model for that provider so newly created agents have something to use.

4. Watch health. Status badge tells you whether the provider is healthy, throttled, or auth-failed.

Typical scenarios:

  · Bringing a new provider online — paste key, test, auto-select, done.
  · Switching from cloud Anthropic to a local proxy by editing the base URL.
  · Diagnosing "why aren't any models available" — usually a missing or expired key here.

Things to know:

  · 401 / 403 from a provider is treated as "reachable, auth-failed" — different from "unreachable" so test results are accurate.
  · Keys are stored in config; treat the host as a secret store.
  · Removing a provider doesn't delete agents that pointed at it — but their model picks become unavailable until re-pointed.
providers.wizard_id_hint: Lowercase with hyphens. Used as the unique identifier.
providers.wizard_api_key_hint: Leave empty for local providers that don't require a key.
providers.wizard_advanced_hint: These values are auto-derived from the Provider ID. Override only if needed.
providers.wizard_models_hint: Optionally add models now, or add them later.
providers.remove_key_confirm_message: This will remove the stored API key and move the provider back to unconfigured.
providers.empty_body: Connect OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Groq, or any other LLM provider so agents can route prompts and consume models.

skills.title: Skills
skills.subtitle: Atomic capabilities that extend agent reasoning.
skills.author: Author
skills.uninstall_confirm: Are you sure you want to uninstall "{{name}}"? This action cannot be undone.
skills.rate_limited_desc: ClawHub API rate limit exceeded. Please try again later.
skills.help:
Skills are atomic capabilities — single-purpose prompt + tool snippets that an agent can pick up to extend its reasoning (e.g. "summarise a long doc", "extract entities", "do a Wikipedia lookup"). Different from hands (managed multi-tool bundles) and plugins (raw code): a skill is a small, declarative unit, often hot-edited.

How to use this page:

1. Browse. Filter by source (built-in, marketplace, custom) and category. Each card shows author, downloads, description.

2. Install / create. Marketplace skills install with one click. "Create" lets you write a custom skill in-place — name, description, prompt; the editor enforces sane limits.

3. Evolve. Each skill has an evolution history — versions, changelogs, attached files. Use it to iterate on the prompt or attach reference docs without losing the prior version.

4. Browse the registry. The registry tab shows the wider public catalogue you can pull from.

Typical scenarios:

  · Installing a community skill that does a tricky thing well, instead of writing it yourself.
  · Forking a skill, editing the prompt for your domain, keeping both versions via evolution.
  · Building a small library of internal skills your agents share.

Things to know:

  · Skill names must pass validation (no path tricks, length cap). Conflicts surface as "name conflict" errors.
  · Prompt size is capped — large reference content goes in evolution-attached files, not the prompt body.
  · Security-blocked errors mean a skill tried to do something the runtime forbids (e.g. write outside its sandbox).

hands.title: Hands
hands.subtitle: Autonomous capability packages.
hands.no_matching_hint: Try a different search term or clear the active filters.
hands.schedule_message_placeholder: What should the hand do when this fires?
hands.help:
Hands are autonomous capability packages — bundled tool sets + glue logic that an agent can pick up to gain a new ability (e.g. "browse the web", "operate Telegram bot", "run sandboxed shell"). Unlike skills (single capabilities) or plugins (raw extensions), a hand is a cohesive, lifecycle-managed bundle.

How to use this page:

1. Browse. Each card shows a hand's name, description, status (active / paused / degraded), and required dependencies.

2. Activate. "Activate" enables the hand — its tools become available to agents that ask for them. "Deactivate" turns it off cleanly.

3. Pause / resume. Pause keeps state but stops the hand from accepting work. Useful when debugging or quota-crunching without losing config. Resume brings it back.

4. Configure delivery. "Configure delivery targets" routes hand outputs to specific channels / agents — the orchestration knob between hand and the rest of the system.

When to use it (vs. Skills / Plugins / MCP):

  · Skills — atomic capability units, smaller-grained.
  · Plugins — raw runtime extensions you wrote or installed.
  · Hands — opinionated, lifecycle-managed capability bundles.
  · MCP — external tool servers reached over a protocol.

Typical scenarios:

  · Activating a "browser" hand so an agent can scrape and summarise pages.
  · Pausing a noisy hand during a quiet period without unconfiguring it.
  · Diagnosing a hand stuck in "degraded" by reading its manifest error.

Things to know:

  · "Degraded" usually means a missing dependency or a failing health-check — the manifest error message points at the cause.
  · Deactivating a hand mid-call is graceful: in-flight invocations finish, no new ones start.
hands.settings_activate_first: Activate this hand first to edit its settings.
hands.uninstall_confirm: Uninstall this hand? Its HAND.toml and workspace files will be deleted. This cannot be undone.

analytics.title: Analytics
analytics.subtitle: Usage statistics and performance telemetry.
analytics.budget_desc: Set spending caps to control costs.
analytics.help:
Analytics aggregates LLM call cost, latency, and throughput across every agent and provider this instance has used. The numbers come from the metering layer that records every model call — same source as the per-agent budget figures, just sliced for charting.

How to use this page:

1. Pick a window. Use the time-range selector to focus on today, this week, this month, or a custom range. All charts and tables re-aggregate.

2. Read the headline metrics. Total cost, total calls, average cost-per-call, average latency — these tell you whether spend is healthy at a glance.

3. Drill in. The "Model performance" table breaks down latency / cost / call count per model so you can see which model is paying for itself. The daily-trend chart shows whether usage is climbing.

4. Set guardrails. The budget panel lets you set hourly / daily / monthly limits and an alert threshold. Crossing the alert threshold surfaces in Approvals.

Typical scenarios:

  · Catching a runaway agent before the monthly bill arrives — daily trend spike + the per-agent ranking on Budget tells you who.
  · Comparing latency between providers when picking a default model.
  · Exporting CSV for finance / reporting.

Things to know:

  · Numbers update as calls land — there's no hourly rollup delay, but very recent calls (last few seconds) may not be counted yet.
  · Cost is computed from the model catalogue's published prices; if a provider's pricing changes, update the model entry on the Models page.
  · Budget limits set here are global; per-agent caps live on the agent's own page.

memory.title: Memory
memory.subtitle: Persistent vector storage for agent context.
memory.config_desc: Changes are written to config.toml. Restart required for full effect.
memory.help:
Memory is the agent's long-term store — embedded vectors plus structured records that the agent retrieves at recall time to keep context across sessions. This page lets you browse, search, edit, and seed memories, and configure the embedding / retrieval behaviour.

How to use this page:

1. Browse / search. The list shows recent memories with access count and metadata. Search runs vector similarity (when an embedding provider is configured) or text fallback.

2. Add. "Add memory" lets you seed a fact manually — pick scope (global / per-agent), fill content, save. Useful for hand-curated knowledge.

3. Edit / delete. Click a row to inspect, edit, or delete. Edits re-embed automatically.

4. Configure. The settings panel toggles auto-memorize (agent writes new memories as it runs), auto-retrieve (memories are injected into context), and chooses the embedding provider / API key.

Typical scenarios:

  · Seeding "always reply in Chinese to user @evan" once, instead of repeating it in the system prompt.
  · Inspecting what an agent has actually remembered and pruning low-value rows.
  · Switching embedding providers when costs / quality change.

Things to know:

  · Storage is SQLite in `~/.librefang/`. Backups live there too.
  · Per-agent memories don't bleed across agents; global ones are visible to all.
  · Disabling auto-retrieve doesn't delete memories — it just stops them being injected. You can still query manually.
memory.api_key_env_hint: Local providers (Ollama / vLLM / LM Studio) typically don't need a key — leave blank.
memory.auto_dream_desc_inline: Periodic memory consolidation per agent. Manifest override > global default; configure global in config.toml under [auto_dream].

comms.subtitle: Real-time message traffic and connectivity.
comms.help:
Comms is the live monitor for the messaging bus. While Channels owns configuration, Comms shows what's actually flowing — connected adapters, recent send/receive events, the agent ↔ channel topology, and basic health.

How to use this page:

1. Health row. Active channels, total events today, daemon uptime — quick "is the bus alive" check.

2. Event timeline. Latest send / receive / error events with source, kind, and timestamp. Search to filter by channel, agent, or text.

3. Topology. Visualises which agents are reachable on which channels right now.

Typical scenarios:

  · "User says the Telegram bot didn't reply" — check the timeline for a matching receive event and whether a send went out.
  · Catching a channel that silently dropped its connection (no heartbeat events).
  · Watching a fresh deploy land — events should resume within seconds.

Things to know:

  · The event stream is in-memory and bounded — older events fall off. For deep history, the audit trail and per-channel logs are authoritative.
  · "Online" here means the adapter is connected; it doesn't guarantee the remote service (Slack, Telegram, …) is healthy upstream.

workflows.title: Workflows
workflows.subtitle: Automation hub.
workflows.select_workflow_hint: Select a workflow to view runs
workflows.delete_confirm: Are you sure you want to delete this workflow?
workflows.help:
Workflows are multi-step automations — a graph of nodes (LLM call, tool call, branch, …) the runtime executes top-to-bottom. Different from a single scheduler fire (one message in, one reply out): a workflow is the recipe with branches and intermediate steps. The visual canvas builds them; templates seed common shapes.

How to use this page:

1. Create. "Create blank" opens an empty canvas, or pick a template ("my workflows" lists yours, "all workflows" shows shared).

2. Edit nodes. Each node has its own context (`ctx_edit`) — edit prompts, tool refs, branch conditions. Connect nodes by dragging.

3. Dry run. "Dry run" validates the workflow without calling LLMs — checks node wiring, missing references, agent IDs. Use it before a live run to catch config errors cheaply.

4. Run now. Provide the run input, click "Run now"; output and resolved prompt show up; "Run history" tracks past runs.

When to use it (vs. Scheduler / Goals):

  · Scheduler — single-shot fire on a clock or event.
  · Goals — long-lived intent, statefully tracked.
  · Workflows — repeatable multi-step recipe with branching.

Typical scenarios:

  · "Pull issue → summarise → ask agent for fix → post comment" as one repeatable workflow.
  · Templating a research pipeline that branches on whether a doc was found.
  · Wiring a workflow as the target of a Scheduler cron — the cron fires the workflow.

Things to know:

  · Dry run catches missing-agent / unresolved-prompt errors without spending tokens — run it before saving big edits.
  · Run history is per-workflow; outputs are kept until you delete the workflow.
  · A failing run leaves a record with the failure point — read it before retrying.

scheduler.title: Scheduler
scheduler.subtitle: Manage cron jobs and scheduled tasks.
scheduler.no_schedules: No schedules yet. Create one to automate agent tasks.
scheduler.help:
Scheduler runs agents on a clock or in response to events. Cron-style schedules fire at fixed intervals; event triggers fire when something happens elsewhere in the system. Each fire posts a message to its target agent (or workflow) just like a human would.

How to use this page:

1. Create a schedule. "Create job" → pick agent or workflow, write a cron expression (or pick "every minute" / "every N hours" presets), set the message body. Save.

2. Event triggers. The events tab lets you bind a trigger to an event source so the agent fires when that event happens, instead of on a clock.

3. Configure delivery. "Edit targets" routes the schedule's output (agent reply) somewhere — a channel, another agent, a webhook.

4. Manage. Toggle active / disabled, see "next run" timestamps, delete old jobs.

When to use it (vs. Goals / Workflows):

  · Goals — long-term intent, statefully tracked.
  · Workflows — multi-step sequences, often graph-shaped.
  · Scheduler — single-shot fires on a clock or event.

Typical scenarios:

  · Daily 9am "summarise yesterday's incidents" message to an ops agent.
  · "Every 15 minutes, check the queue and act if depth > N".
  · Event-driven fire when a webhook or another agent sends an event.

Things to know:

  · All cron fires for an agent share one session — they don't isolate per-fire. To get per-fire isolation, use `session_mode = "new"` AND don't rely on cron (use triggers via the dispatcher path).
  · Cron fires are subject to per-agent concurrency caps. A long-running job will block the next fire.
  · Cron expressions are second-precision when supported by the parser; check the next-run preview before saving.

delivery.no_targets: No fan-out targets. The legacy single delivery still applies.
delivery.channel_type_helper: Custom channel types are passed through as-is. Unknown values fail at delivery time with the list of configured adapters.

runtime.title: Runtime
runtime.subtitle: Engine status and resource management.
runtime.shutdown_confirm_desc: The server will stop and the dashboard will become unavailable.
runtime.help:
Runtime is the engine-level dashboard — kernel health, build info, listen addresses, queue / lane status, audit chain status, backups, and lifecycle controls. Use it to answer "is the daemon healthy and what's it doing right now?"

How to use this page:

1. Health row. Active agents, channels, audit entries, uptime, build time, auth mode — all the at-a-glance facts.

2. Audit verify. "Audit verify" walks the hash chain and reports valid / invalid. Run after suspicious events or before evidence export.

3. Backup. "Create backup" snapshots the daemon's state directory. Backups list shows what you have available.

4. Lifecycle. Reload config (re-reads config.toml, applies hot-reloadable knobs), cleanup sessions (drops idle session state), shut down (stops the daemon — restart externally).

Typical scenarios:

  · Post-config-change: hit Reload, watch new values take effect without restart.
  · Pre-deploy or pre-incident: take a backup so you can roll back state.
  · Confirming the audit chain is unbroken before exporting it for review.

Things to know:

  · Reload only picks up hot-reloadable fields — some changes still need a full restart (look for "needs restart" hints next to fields).
  · Cleanup-sessions is non-destructive: it drops in-memory caches, not stored history.
  · Some per-agent caps (e.g. `max_concurrent_invocations`) are NOT picked up on reload; the agent must respawn.
runtime.load_error_desc: Core runtime data could not be loaded. Retry after the daemon and API health checks recover.
runtime.restore_success: Restore complete. Restart daemon for changes.
runtime.restore_confirm_desc: Current runtime state will be replaced with data from this backup.
runtime.delete_backup_confirm_desc: This backup file will be permanently removed.

telemetry.title: Telemetry
telemetry.subtitle: System metrics, LLM usage, and HTTP observability.
telemetry.help:
Telemetry is the systems-level observability surface — uptime, panics, restarts, request rates, error rates, active sessions, LLM token totals, and the top HTTP endpoints. Numbers come from the same Prometheus endpoint the daemon exposes; this page just renders them.

How to use this page:

1. Health section. Uptime, panics, restarts — has the daemon been stable.

2. Activity section. Active agents, sessions, total LLM calls, today's cost — what's happening right now.

3. Reliability section. Total requests, error rate, top endpoints — is the API serving traffic cleanly.

4. View raw. "View raw" opens the underlying Prometheus text endpoint for ad-hoc scraping or manual debug.

Typical scenarios:

  · Daily morning glance: uptime fine, no panics, error rate normal.
  · Diagnosing slowness: top-endpoints list points to the offender.
  · Wiring an external Prometheus / Grafana — point it at the same endpoint linked from "View raw".

Things to know:

  · Numbers are live counters; restarts reset most of them. The Prometheus endpoint is the long-term store if you scrape externally.
  · "Cost today" is local-day in the daemon's timezone.
  · Per-agent breakdown lives on Analytics / Budget; this page is system-wide.
telemetry.no_data: No telemetry data yet. Make some API requests to see metrics.

logs.title: Logs
logs.subtitle: Real-time diagnostic events.
logs.help:
Logs is the live diagnostic stream — structured log events from the daemon's tracing layer, filterable by module and searchable by text. Use it for "what is the runtime saying right now" debugging; for tamper-evident history use the Audit trail instead.

How to use this page:

1. Filter by module. The dropdown narrows to one subsystem (kernel / runtime / channel / …). "All modules" shows everything.

2. Search. Free-text match against the message body — useful for grepping for an agent id, a channel name, or an error string.

3. Export JSON. Dump the current view to JSON for sharing with support or feeding into external log tooling.

When to use it (vs. Audit trail):

  · Logs — diagnostic, fine-grained, ephemeral. Read for "what's happening right now".
  · Audit trail — privileged actions only, hash-chained, durable. Read for "who did what and when".

Typical scenarios:

  · Watching an agent boot and confirming each subsystem registered.
  · Grepping logs for a request id when chasing a 5xx.
  · Capturing logs around an incident and exporting JSON for a bug report.

Things to know:

  · Log volume is bounded — old events fall off. Run an external collector (or scrape Prometheus) for long retention.
  · Module-level filter is server-side; search is client-side over what's loaded.
  · Logs are not redacted automatically — be careful when exporting if your messages may contain secrets or PII.

settings.help:
Dashboard preferences and account-level controls. This is the place to change your local UI settings, manage TOTP, and trigger Auto-Dream runs — it does not edit kernel configuration on disk (that's the Config page).

What lives on this page:

  · UI preferences — language (English / 中文), theme (light / dark), sidebar collapse default. Stored in browser localStorage; per-device.
  · TOTP enrollment — protect destructive actions (approvals, schedule deletes, plugin install) with a 6-digit one-time code. Setup gives you a QR code and recovery codes; revoke removes the requirement.
  · Auto-Dream — periodic background pass where the agent reviews its own conversation log and emits memory updates. Trigger one manually, watch a running pass, or enable the daily schedule.

Settings (this page) vs. Config:

  · Settings — per-user, per-browser, lightweight UI / account toggles.
  · Config (/dashboard/config) — kernel-level TOML editor: providers, channels, MCP servers, plugin policies, runtime caps. Changes there require kernel reload.

Things to know:

  · TOTP secrets are stored encrypted in the kernel vault. If you lose both your authenticator and recovery codes, an admin has to reset by editing config + redeploying.
  · Auto-Dream runs in the kernel under the agent's own session, so it counts toward that agent's budget. The "Run now" button is gated by TOTP if you have it enrolled.
  · Theme / language changes are instant. The kernel does not need a reload.
settings.export_config_desc: Download your current configuration as a TOML file.

goals.title: Goals
goals.subtitle: Strategic planning and task tracking.
goals.delete_confirm: Are you sure you want to delete this goal?
goals.pick_template_desc: Pick a template to get started quickly with pre-defined goals.
goals.help:
Goals is the strategic-planning surface — a tree of objectives the agent (or you) is working towards, with progress, status, and parent / child relationships. Templates seed common goal shapes; the tree view shows hierarchy and roll-up progress.

How to use this page:

1. Create. "Create goal" opens a form for title, description, parent goal, and status. Or "Pick template" to apply a pre-built goal tree (e.g. "ship a feature", "research a topic").

2. Track. Each node shows pending / in-progress / completed. Parent progress aggregates from children. Edit a node inline to flip its state or refine its description.

3. Prune. "Clear all" wipes the tree (with a confirm) — useful when starting a new initiative.

When to use it (vs. Scheduler / Workflows):

  · Goals — what we want. Long-lived intentions, often agent-readable.
  · Scheduler / Workflows — how it gets done. Concrete fires and steps.

Typical scenarios:

  · Giving an agent a long-term direction it can refer back to during planning.
  · Tracking a multi-week project as a tree the human and the agent both see.

Things to know:

  · Templates are starting points — feel free to edit the tree after applying.
  · Deleting a parent deletes its children. Confirm before "Clear all".

sessions.title: Sessions
sessions.subtitle: Active agent conversations and historical execution contexts.
sessions.empty_desc: Sessions will appear when agents start conversations.
sessions.help:
Sessions are the live and historical conversation contexts of every agent. Each session is a thread of messages + tool calls bound to one agent and one origin (channel, cron, A2A peer, or manual). This page lets you browse them, label them, and clean up.

How to use this page:

1. Search & filter. Type to search by agent, label, or recent message text. Active vs. idle is auto-derived from last activity.

2. Label. "Set label" tags a session so it's easier to find later — useful for long-running threads.

3. Delete. Remove old sessions you don't need. The agent's full message history for that thread goes with it.

Typical scenarios:

  · Finding the session where the agent and a user worked through a specific problem.
  · Pruning channel sessions that have piled up after a busy week.
  · Confirming an agent has the session you expect for a given (channel, chat) pair before debugging.

Things to know:

  · Channel messages always use a canonical session id — `(agent, "<channel>:<chat>")` — so a user always lands in the same thread.
  · Cron fires share a single per-agent session; that's by design (prompt cache reuse) but it does mean cron history can grow.
  · Deleting a session is irreversible. Memory entries the session created stay (Memory page is separate).
sessions.open_in_chat: Open this session in the chat page

approvals.title: Approvals
approvals.subtitle: Review and authorize critical agent actions.
approvals.totpRequired: TOTP code required. Use the Approvals page.
approvals.help:
Approvals is the human-in-the-loop queue. When an agent invokes a tool that's flagged as "requires approval" (destructive shell commands, large-spend LLM calls, sensitive HTTP, etc.) the call pauses and a request lands here. You approve, modify, or reject; the agent resumes with that decision.

How to use this page:

1. Triage. Each card shows the agent, the tool, the arguments, and the reason approval was required. Use the filters to narrow by status (pending / approved / rejected / expired).

2. Decide. "Approve" lets the call go through as-is. "Modify" lets you edit the arguments before approving (e.g. tighten the SQL WHERE clause). "Reject" cancels the call and returns an error to the agent.

3. Bulk-handle. Select multiple pending requests and approve / reject in batch. Batch approve is disabled if you have TOTP enrolled (every approve must enter a fresh code).

4. Enroll TOTP. Use the TOTP setup flow if your policy requires a second factor on approvals. Once enrolled, single approvals require a 6-digit code; batch approval is locked off.

Typical scenarios:

  · Letting an agent autonomously edit code but gating any `rm -rf` / `git push` behind a human eye.
  · Catching the first time an agent tries to spend > $X on a single LLM call.
  · Reviewing a queue of channel-bot replies before they go out, during a soak-test phase.

Things to know:

  · Pending requests have a TTL — if you don't decide in time they expire and the agent gets a "rejected by timeout" answer. The TTL is configurable in approvals policy.
  · Modify is auditable: the diff between original and modified args is recorded.
  · TOTP is per-operator. Enrolment and revocation are also audit-logged.

models.title: Model Catalog
models.subtitle: Browse all available LLM models across providers.
models.no_models: No models loaded. Is the daemon running?

models.help:
The model catalogue is this instance's view of every LLM model available across configured providers — IDs, capabilities (tools / vision / streaming / thinking), context window, input / output pricing, and tier. The catalogue feeds the model picker on every agent and the cost calculator on Analytics.

How to use this page:

1. Filter & sort. Filter by provider, tier, or "available only" (excludes models whose provider isn't configured). Search by name / alias.

2. Compare. Columns side-by-side: context, input price, output price, tools, vision, thinking. Pick what fits the workload before assigning to an agent.

3. Add custom. "Add custom model" lets you register a model the catalogue doesn't ship with — useful for new releases or self-hosted endpoints. Provide ID, display name, provider, and pricing.

4. Edit / delete. Tweak display name or pricing on existing entries. Deletion is for custom entries you added.

Typical scenarios:

  · Picking a vision-capable model for a screenshot-analysis agent.
  · Registering a brand-new model the day it launches, before LibreFang ships an update.
  · Auditing which models have tool-use vs. which don't, before assigning agents.

Things to know:

  · "Available" depends on whether the provider is configured and reachable. Configure it on Providers first.
  · Pricing here is what the cost calculator believes — keep it accurate or budget figures drift.
  · Aliases let you address one underlying model under multiple names (e.g. "smart" → claude-opus).
models.capabilities_override_hint: Override capabilities when the provider's metadata is wrong, missing, or non-standard. "Auto" uses the catalog default.

media.title: Media Generation
media.subtitle: Generate images, speech, videos, and music through configured providers.
media.help:
Media generation is the cross-provider front-end for images, speech, video, and music. All endpoints share the same provider abstraction — pick a capability, optionally pick a provider, prompt, generate.

How to use this page:

1. Pick a capability. Image / TTS / video / music tabs share the same shape: prompt, parameters, generate.

2. Provider auto-detect. Leave provider on auto and the backend picks the first configured driver for that capability. Override only when you want a specific one.

3. Tune parameters. Aspect ratio, count, format (wav / opus / flac / …), instrumental vs. vocal — each capability exposes the relevant knobs.

4. Generate & download. Output appears inline; "Download" saves the file locally.

Typical scenarios:

  · Quick one-off image / voice generation without leaving the dashboard.
  · Comparing the same prompt across providers by switching the provider drop-down.
  · Generating short audio clips for use in a channel bot reply.

Things to know:

  · Cost lands on the same metering as LLM calls — large videos / music can be expensive.
  · If "auto" returns "no provider configured", set one up on Providers first.
  · Outputs live in the daemon's media dir; the dashboard streams them, but the source of truth is on disk.

media.video_polling: Task in progress, polling every 5 seconds...
media.music_desc: Compose music from a style prompt or lyrics.

media.no_provider_video: No provider configured for video generation
media.no_provider_music: No provider configured for music generation

network.title: Network
network.subtitle: Open Federation Protocol network status and connected peers.
network.no_peers_desc: No OFP peers are currently connected to this node.
network.identity_fingerprint_hint: Compare with the remote operator out-of-band before pinning.
network.identity_missing: No Ed25519 identity loaded. OFP is running in HMAC-only legacy mode; per-peer impersonation defense (#3873) is dormant.
network.trusted_peer_fingerprint_hint: SHA-256 fingerprint of the pinned Ed25519 public key. Compare OOB.
network.no_trusted_peers_desc: No peers have been TOFU-pinned to this node's identity yet. Pin a peer's fingerprint to enable per-peer impersonation defense (#3873).
network.ofp_disabled: OFP networking is not enabled. Set `network_enabled = true` and a non-empty `shared_secret` in config.toml. The Ed25519 identity is generated and persisted automatically on first start.
network.help:
Network shows this instance's status in the Open Federation Protocol (OFP) — peer LibreFang nodes you've discovered or been discovered by, your own node identity, and the OFP version in use. OFP is the layer below A2A: it's how nodes find each other; A2A is what they say once connected.

How to use this page:

1. Identity. The node-id and protocol version identify this LibreFang instance to the federation.

2. Peers. Shows every connected peer with online / offline status. Empty list means you're not federating with anyone yet.

3. Status. Online means OFP is reachable and your node is announcing itself.

When to use it (vs. A2A):

  · Network — node-level federation: who's out there, who's connected.
  · A2A — agent-level talk: what agents on those nodes can do for each other.

Typical scenarios:

  · Confirming two LibreFang instances actually see each other before debugging A2A.
  · Reading off your node-id to share with a peer for direct connection.

Things to know:

  · Federation is opt-in and config-gated. If "no peers" looks empty by default, that's expected unless you've configured peers.
  · Network status is a live read — refresh polls, no caching.

a2a.title: A2A
a2a.subtitle: Discover and communicate with external A2A agents.
a2a.no_agents_desc: Discover A2A agents by entering their URL.
a2a.task_placeholder: Describe the task for this agent...
a2a.help:
A2A (Agent-to-Agent) is an open protocol from Google that lets independent agent runtimes discover and call each other. Every A2A agent publishes an "agent card" at /.well-known/agent.json describing its name, skills, and how to send it tasks. LibreFang serves its own card at the same path, so other A2A clients can discover this instance too.

How to use this page:

1. Discover. Click "Discover" (shortcut: n) and paste the URL of an external agent (e.g. https://example.com/agents/research-bot). LibreFang fetches /.well-known/agent.json and adds it to "External agents" with its skills and availability.

2. Send a task. On any agent card, click "Send task", type a natural-language instruction, and submit. LibreFang sends a JSON-RPC task and gets back a task_id. The new task appears under "Tracked tasks".

3. Track. Click "Refresh" on a tracked task to poll its status: pending → completed / failed. The result text shows up inline when the remote agent finishes.

When to use it (vs. Channels and MCP):

  · Channels — a person talks to your agent via Telegram / Slack / etc. Direction: human → agent.
  · MCP — your agent calls an external tool server. Direction: agent → tool.
  · A2A — your agent talks to someone else's agent as a peer. Direction: agent ↔ agent.

Typical scenarios:

  · Two LibreFang instances (yours + a teammate's, or home + work) cooperating on tasks.
  · Subscribing to a public A2A agent that specialises in something — e.g. a research / translation / search agent — and forwarding suitable tasks to it.
  · Splitting a fleet of internal LibreFang instances by domain expertise and routing between them.

Things to know:

  · The "Tracked tasks" list lives only in your browser — refreshing the page drops it. The remote side keeps its own task store; save the task_id if you need to look it up later.
  · LibreFang's SSRF protection blocks discovery of localhost / private-network URLs by default. To discover an agent on your LAN, add its host to ssrf_allowed_hosts in config.toml.
  · The A2A ecosystem is still young — public A2A endpoints are rare today. The most concrete way to try this page is to point another LibreFang instance at this one and discover both ways.

plugins.title: Plugins
plugins.subtitle: Extend agent capabilities with custom plugins.
plugins.no_plugins_desc: Install plugins from the marketplace or create your own.
plugins.help:
Plugins are runtime extensions — code you (or someone else) wrote that hooks into the kernel and adds tools, hooks, or behaviour beyond what ships in core. Different from skills (declarative capability docs) and hands (managed bundles): a plugin is raw code with full access to the runtime.

How to use this page:

1. Browse. The "Installed" tab lists active plugins with version, hooks, dependencies, size, and metadata. The marketplace tab lists ones you can install.

2. Install. "Install" pulls from the marketplace, a local path, or a Git repository / branch. Dependency install runs as part of the flow; the result panel shows success or the failing step.

3. Inspect. Click a plugin to see its hooks, deps, and metadata. Useful when debugging which plugin owns a tool name.

4. Remove. Delete uninstalls — removes the plugin code and unhooks anything it registered.

Typical scenarios:

  · Adding a custom plugin that exposes your internal API as agent tools.
  · Pulling a community plugin from a Git URL, branch-pinned to a known-good commit.
  · Removing a plugin that conflicts with another for the same tool name.

Things to know:

  · Plugins run with daemon privileges — only install ones you trust.
  · Installing from Git locks to a branch; redeploy by re-installing if the upstream branch moves.
  · A failing dependency install leaves the plugin in an invalid state — the panel shows what's missing.

mcp.title: MCP Servers
mcp.subtitle: Manage Model Context Protocol server connections
mcp.empty_desc: Add an MCP server to extend agent capabilities with external tools.
mcp.delete_confirm: Are you sure you want to delete this MCP server? This action cannot be undone.
mcp.name: Name
mcp.help:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are external tool providers your agents can call. The protocol standardises tool-discovery and tool-invocation, so any MCP-compliant server becomes available to agents on this instance once added.

Supported transports:
  · stdio — runs a local command (e.g. `npx`, `python`)
  · SSE — connects via Server-Sent Events
  · HTTP — connects via HTTP Streamable

How to use this page:

1. Install from catalogue. Browse the catalogue tab and click "Install" on a known server. The transport, args, and env are pre-filled.

2. Add custom. "Add server" lets you wire any MCP server — pick the transport, provide the command / URL, fill args and env vars.

3. Authenticate. Servers that need OAuth show "Authorize"; click it to start the PKCE flow. Re-authorize when tokens expire; revoke to cut access.

4. Verify. After add / connect, the server card lists its tools. Agents can now request them via tool policy.

Typical scenarios:

  · Plugging in a filesystem MCP so agents can read and write a sandbox dir.
  · Wiring a SaaS MCP (Notion, Linear, …) for ticket / doc workflows.
  · Running an internal MCP server that exposes your business APIs.

Things to know:

  · OAuth flow is UI-driven — daemon detects 401, the API server runs PKCE and the callback. Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) is used when the server supports it.
  · Env-var values are stored as configured; treat them as secrets.
  · A failing server keeps showing in the list as auth-pending / failed; check the auth and transport before debugging tools.

audit.title: Audit trail
audit.subtitle: Searchable, filterable audit log across users / actions / agents.
audit.help:
The audit trail is a hash-chained, tamper-evident log of every privileged action this instance has taken — agent spawns, approvals, config changes, RBAC edits, key rotations, etc. Each row is linked to the previous via a SHA hash, so any retroactive edit to old rows breaks the chain and is detectable.

How to use this page:

1. Filter. Narrow by user, agent, action type, channel, or time range. Filters operate on an in-memory window (server hard cap 5000 rows); deeper history needs an export.

2. Inspect. Click a row to see the full payload, hash, previous-hash, and timestamp. Copy hash / JSON / link-to-row from the detail panel for incident reports.

3. Verify. The Runtime page has an "Audit verify" button that walks the chain and reports valid / invalid. Run it after a suspicious event or before exporting evidence.

4. Export. CSV is for reporting; JSON keeps the full payload + hashes so you can verify the chain offline (or feed it into an external SIEM).

Typical scenarios:

  · Post-incident: who approved the destructive call at 03:14 UTC, with what arguments?
  · Compliance: monthly export of all RBAC and config changes.
  · Forensics: detect that a row was tampered with, by re-verifying the chain offline.

Things to know:

  · The hard 5000-row in-memory cap is a hot-window guardrail, not the storage limit. Older rows are still on disk; export pulls from disk.
  · Filters narrow the displayed rows but don't change the chain — verification always runs over the full chain.
  · Don't redact rows in-place. Hash chain integrity depends on every byte of every row staying byte-identical to when it was written.
audit.forbidden_body: /api/audit/query is admin-only. Sign in with an Admin or Owner api_key.
audit.empty_filtered: Try widening the filters, or clear them to see the most recent rows.
audit.empty_unfiltered: Nothing recorded yet. As soon as agents take privileged actions they appear here.

users.title: Users & RBAC
users.subtitle: Manage operator accounts, channel bindings, and bulk-onboard via CSV.
users.help:
Users & RBAC manages operator accounts and external identities — every row is a person or a service account, with one or more channel bindings (Telegram / Discord / Slack / email) mapped to a LibreFang role (admin / operator / viewer / …). API keys for programmatic access are issued here too. Admin-only.

How to use this page:

1. Create. "Create user" → fill ID, name, role; optionally add channel bindings and an API key. Save.

2. Edit. Click a row to update role, add / remove bindings, set per-user budget / memory / policy hints (badges on the row show which are set).

3. Bulk onboard. "Paste CSV" lets you import many users at once — useful for first-day onboarding of a team.

4. Rotate / revoke. Rotate API key with the rotate flow (old key invalidated immediately). Delete removes the user and all their bindings.

Typical scenarios:

  · Adding a teammate so their Slack DMs route as themselves with the right role.
  · Issuing an API key to a CI bot for programmatic agent calls.
  · Bulk onboarding a class / cohort via CSV.

Things to know:

  · Rotate is destructive to in-flight clients — they get 401 immediately. Coordinate before rotating production keys.
  · Channel bindings are unique per (channel, identity) — the same Telegram id can't map to two LibreFang users.
  · Per-user policy / budget overrides are stored separately; the badges on the row tell you which a user has.

user_budget.title: User budget
user_budget.zero_means_unlimited: Set any window to 0 for unlimited on that window. Threshold is the fraction of any limit at which a BudgetExceeded audit fires.
user_budget.help:
Per-user spend cap. Anything one user does — across every agent, every channel — counts against this cap. Set caps here when you want to give a teammate access to your LibreFang instance without them being able to drain your provider credit.

How budgets stack:

  · Global budget (set in /dashboard/runtime) — kernel-wide ceiling. Hits this and every agent stops.
  · Per-agent budget (set in agent.toml) — caps a specific agent's spend.
  · Per-user budget (this page) — caps a specific user's combined spend.
  · The most restrictive limit wins. If global has $5 left and the user has $50 left, the user can spend $5.

What you see here:

  · Current spend (this period), the cap, percent used, and the rolling window (daily / weekly / monthly).
  · "alert_breach" badge appears when the user has crossed an alert threshold but is still under the hard cap.
  · Per-provider breakdown: which providers contributed how much to the user's spend.

Things to know:

  · A user with no budget configured is unlimited (still subject to global / per-agent caps).
  · Edits take effect on the next request the user makes — already-running LLM calls continue.
  · Spend totals are persisted in SQLite; deleting the user does not delete history (audit trail).

user_policy.title: Permission matrix
user_policy.load_error_body: The daemon returned an error fetching the per-user policy slice.
user_policy.tool_policy_desc: Per-user allow + deny patterns. Layered ON TOP of agent + channel rules. Deny wins.
user_policy.glob_hint: One pattern per line. Glob with `*` allowed.
user_policy.categories_desc: Bulk allow / deny by tool group name (defined in `KernelConfig.tool_policy.groups`).
user_policy.memory_desc: Namespace ACL + PII redaction toggles. Writable must be a subset of readable.
user_policy.ns_hint: One namespace per line. `*` matches all.
user_policy.writable_hint: Must be a subset of readable.
user_policy.channels_desc: Override the user's tool surface per channel adapter (telegram / discord / …). Add custom adapters with the input below.
user_policy.add_channel_hint: Channel adapter name (lowercase). Must match the key the bridge emits.
user_policy.add_channel_err_duplicate: Channel '{{key}}' already has a rule slot.
user_policy.help:
The full RBAC picture for one user: which tools they can call, which categories are allowed, which channel rules apply to them, and which agents they can talk to. Read-only summary — to change values, edit the user via the Users page or the kernel config.

Three slices contribute to every gate decision:

  · Per-user tool_policy / tool_categories — overrides on this page.
  · Per-agent ToolPolicy — set in agent.toml.
  · Per-channel rules — set in [channels.<name>] config.

How to use:

  1. Pick the user (header). The page is scoped to that user across all sections.
  2. Each slice shows: configured rules + "not configured" markers (which mean "fall through to the next layer").
  3. Use the Permission Simulator (/dashboard/permission-simulator) to see what the gate decides for a specific tool call once all three slices intersect.

Permission matrix (this page) vs. Permission simulator:

  · Matrix — raw configured slices, no decision logic.
  · Simulator — runs the actual intersection for a specific (user, agent, tool, channel) tuple and tells you the verdict.

Things to know:

  · "Not configured" never means "denied". It means "defer to other layers". A user with no tool_policy is still bound by per-agent and per-channel rules.
  · Channel rules are evaluated only when the request comes via that channel; web-dashboard and CLI calls bypass channel rules.
  · This page is read-only. Edits are at /dashboard/users → click the user → edit modal.

simulator.title: Permission simulator
simulator.subtitle: Pick a user and see every RBAC input contributing to their permissions.
simulator.empty_desc: Add a user from the Users page first.
simulator.not_found_desc: The selected user is not registered with the AuthManager. They may have been removed from config.toml since the user list cached.
simulator.help:
The permission simulator picks a user and shows every RBAC input that contributes to their effective permissions — role, tool policy, allowed / denied tool lists, allowed / denied groups, channel rules and bindings, memory namespaces, budget caps, PII access, delete / export permissions. It's a debug surface for "why does (or doesn't) this user have access to X".

How to use this page:

1. Pick a user. Use the chooser; the page populates with their slices.

2. Read each section. Every section shows the RAW configured slice for that user — role matrix, tool policy, channel bindings, memory permissions, budget, etc. "Not configured" means that layer is silent for this user.

3. Compare with reality. The runtime intersects these slices at call time; this page does NOT show the per-call gate decision, only the inputs.

When to use it (vs. running the actual agent):

  · Simulator — fast static view of every input, no LLM call needed.
  · Actual agent call — the only ground truth for what runs / doesn't.

Typical scenarios:

  · "Why can't user @bob delete memories?" — section shows delete_off.
  · "Did the new tool policy actually land for @alice?" — see allowed / denied lists.
  · Auditing channel rule coverage before granting a new external user access.

Things to know:

  · Sections show RAW slices, not the gate decision. The runtime gate intersects per-agent ToolPolicy + per-user tool_policy / tool_categories + per-channel rules; "Not configured" defers to other layers.
  · The simulator reads current config — changes you make on Users / Policy pages take effect immediately on refresh.
simulator.tool_policy_unset: Defers to per-agent ToolPolicy and channel rules.
simulator.tool_categories_unset: No category-level overrides for this user.
simulator.memory_unset: Falls back to the role-default ACL (Owner/Admin = full, User = proactive + kv:*, Viewer = proactive read-only).
simulator.budget_unset: No per-user cap. Bounded by global / per-agent / per-provider budgets only.
simulator.bindings_unset: No platform IDs bound. Inbound from any channel will be unrecognised.
simulator.channel_rules_unset: No per-channel overrides. The global ApprovalPolicy.channel_rules still applies.
